source_metadata.title_raw: Peter Douglas - A-82-046 - February 26, 1982 - San Francisco

content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 207 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1120 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Inter
(916) 322-5660      322-5660      322-3901      322-5441      322-6444

February 26, 1982


Peter Douglas
Chief Deputy Director
California Coastal Commission
531 Howard Street, 4th Floor
San Francisco, CA 94105

RE: Lynn Heacox
     FPPC No. GC-81/1265

Dear Mr. Douglas:

     We have completed our evaluation of the potential Political Reform Act (the Act) violations you referred to in your December 15, 1981, letter to Lynn Heacox. In that letter, you expressed concern that Mr. Heacox, a former permit analyst for the California Coastal Commission, may have violated Sections 87401 and/or 87402 of the Act (Government Code Sections 87401, 87402).1/ Those provisions generally prohibit a former state employee from representing a private party before a state agency regarding a matter on which the former employee worked while employed by the state. In order for there to have been a violation of those sections, however, the former employee must be compensated for his representation by the private party. Our evaluation of Mr. Heacox's representation activities in question has determined that he received no compensation from the private parties involved. Therefore his actions thus far in regard to those parties' building permit applications before the California Coastal Commission do not constitute violations of the Act. Nevertheless, Mr. Heacox has indicated to us that he wishes to do compensated work for one of the private parties by presenting a new permit application to the Commission on behalf of that party. For his benefit and yours we therefore offer the following advice as to when a former permit analyst should not accept employment with private parties who have matters pending before the California Coastal Commission.


1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted.

Peter Douglas
February 26, 1982
Page 2


Taken together, Sections 87401 and 87402 prevent a former state employee, after leaving state employment, from acting as the representative for or assisting in the representation of a person in a state agency proceeding in which the former state employee "participated" while a state employee. "Participated" is defined in Section 87400(d) as meaning "to have taken part personally and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation or use of confidential information as an officer or employee . . ." The general purposes of the prohibitions of Sections 87401 and 87402 are contained in a February 21, 1979, memorandum to the Fair Political Practices Commissioners from the Commission staff. The memorandum contained a proposed draft of Sections 87400-87405 stating that these new statutes were attempting to embody certain broad principles of policy:

     1.  The interests of the government and the public as a former client should be protected by preventing the disclosure or use of matters revealed by reason of a relationship having confidential aspects.

     2.  The integrity of the governmental decision-making process should be protected by reducing the chance that post-government employment considerations will bias the conduct of government business.

     3.  Both the interests of the government and the public and the integrity of the governmental decision-making process should be protected by limitations on the opportunity for undue advantage by reason of familiarity with agency practice and procedure and personal relationships with agency personnel.

Against these principles must be balanced:

     1.  The ability of the government to recruit competent personnel whose career objectives will require an assessment of the disadvantages of government service.

     2.  The advantage to efficient government operations of having participants in governmental proceedings who are acquainted with practice, procedure, and precedent.

Peter Douglas
February 26, 1982
Page 3


3. The right of private parties to obtain
competent representatives of their own choice.

As a general rule, therefore, a former California
Coastal Commission permit analyst may not accept employment
with a private party in regard to a development site permit
application, or other related proceeding, pending before the
California Coastal Commission when the former permit analyst
has "personally and substantially participated" in the prior
permit history for development on that particular site. As
noted above, the term "personally and substantially" is used
in Section 87400(d) to describe a state employee's participation in matters reviewed by his state agency employer. The
types of activities that usually will constitute personal and
substantial involvement by a permit analyst are described in
the State Personnel Board's specifications sheet for "Coastal
Program Analyst" (permit analyst):

     Work assignments involve planning, regulating,
and controlling development of the coast; advising
permit applicants on the planning, technical and
legal aspects of their proposed projects, consulting with local, regional, State, Federal planning
officials and with the public to coordinate and
integrate regulatory and planning activities;
determining the conformity of proposed coastal development with standards of the California Coastal
Act of 1976; assisting local governments in the
preparation of local coastal programs required by
the Coastal Act; reviewing local coastal programs
to determine whether they have been developed and
are being carried out in accordance with State and
Federal laws; and preparing recommendations to the
State Coastal Commission and six regional Coastal
Commissions to guide the Commissions to their formal actions on development permits and appeals,
coastal planning, and local coastal programs.

This list is not necessarily exhaustive of all the permit history activities in which a permit analyst may personally and substantially engage that would limit his future
private employment opportunities. Moreover there may be
exceptions where participation in one of the above work
assignments for a particular project would not prevent
future private employment on the same project. See Section 87403.

Peter Douglas
February 26, 1982
Page 4


Thus since the prohibitions of Sections 87401 and 87402
require a subjective test to be applied in determining possi-
ble violations, each situation must be reviewed on an indi-
vidual basis. Former California Coastal Commission employees
therefore should contact the California Coastal Commission
legal staff for guidance as to what specific permit analyst
activities constitute personal and substantial participation
for purposes of Sections 87401 and 87402. The
Coastal Commission then may look to the PPPC's Legal Division
for assistance in interpreting the intent and definitions of
the terms of these sections. Weighing the facts of each case
in light of the broad policy considerations set forth above
should lead to a correct and fair determination.

     If I can be of any further help in this particular
matter, please do not hesitate to contact me.

                              Sincerely,

                              [signature]
                              Steven R. Crooks
                              Legal Counsel
                              Enforcement Division

February 26, 1982


Peter Douglas
Chief Deputy Director
California Coastal Commission
531 Howard Street, 4th Floor
San Francisco, CA 94105

RE: Lynn Heacox
     FPPC No. GC-81/1265

Dear Mr. Douglas:

We have completed our evaluation of the potential Polit-
ical Reform Act (the Act) violations you referred to in your
December 15, 1981, letter to Lynn Heacox. In that letter,
you expressed concern that Mr. Heacox, a former permit ana-
lyst for the California Coastal Commission, may have violated
Sections 87401 and/or 87402 of the Act (Government Code
Sections 87401, 87402)1/. Those provisions generally pro-
hibit a former state employee from representing a private
party before a state agency regarding a matter on which the
former employee worked while employed by the state. In order
for there to have been a violation of those sections,
however, the former employee must be compensated for his rep-
resentation by the private party. Our evaluation of Mr.
Heacox's representation activities in question has determined
that he received no compensation from the private parties
involved. Therefore his actions thus far in regard to those
parties' building permit applications before the California
Coastal Commission do not constitute violations of the Act.
Nevertheless, Mr. Heacox has indicated to us that he wishes
to do compensated work for one of the private parties by
presenting a new permit application to the Commission on be-
half of that party. For his benefit and yours we therefore
offer the following advice as to when a former permit analyst
should not accept employment with private parties who have
matters pending before the California Coastal Commission.


1/ All statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

Peter Douglas
February 26, 1982
Page 2


Taken together, Sections 87401 and 87402 prevent a for-
mer state employee, after leaving state employment, from
acting as the representative for or assisting in the repre-
sentation of a person in a state agency proceeding in which
the former state employee "participated" while a state
employee. "Participated" is defined in Section 87401(d) as
meaning "to have taken part personally and substantially
through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis,
investigation or use of confidential information as an offi-
cer or employee . . . ." The general purposes of the
prohibitions of Sections 87401 and 87402 are examined in a
February 21, 1979, memorandum to the Fair Political Practices
Commissioners from the Commission staff. The memorandum con-
tained a proposed draft of Sections 87400-87405 stating that
these new statutes were attempting to embody certain broad
principles of policy:

     1. The interests of the government and the
public as a former client should be protected
by preventing the disclosure or use of matters
revealed by reason of a relationship having
confidential aspects.

     2. The integrity of the governmental
decision-making process should be protected by
reducing the chance that post-government em-
ployment considerations will bias the conduct
of government business.

     3. Both the interests of the government and
the public and the integrity of the
governmental decision-making process should be
protected by limitations on the opportunity
for undue advantage by reason of familiarity
with agency practice and procedure and person-
al relationships with agency personnel.

Against these principles must be balanced:

     1. The ability of the government to recruit
competent personnel whose career objectives
will require an assessment of the
disadvantages of government service.

     2. The advantage to efficient government
operations of having participants in
governmental proceedings who are acquainted
with practice, procedure, and precedent.

Peter Douglas
February 26, 1982
Page 3


     3.  The right of private parties to obtain
competent representatives of their own choice.

     As a general rule, therefore, a former California
Coastal Commission permit analyst may not accept employment
with a private party in regard to a development site permit
application, or other related proceeding, pending before the
California Coastal Commission when the former permit analyst
has "personally and substantially participated" in the prior
permit history for development on that particular site. As
noted above, the term "personally and substantially" is used
in Section 87400(d) to describe a state employee's participa-
tion in matters reviewed by his state agency employer. The
types of activities that usually will constitute personal and
substantial involvement by a permit analyst are described in
the State Personnel Board's specifications sheet for "Coastal
Program Analyst," (permit analyst):

     Work assignments involve planning, regulating,
and controlling development of the coast; advising
permit applicants on the planning, technical and
legal aspects of their proposed projects, consult-
ing with local, regional, State, Federal planning
officials and with the public to coordinate and
integrate regulatory and planning activities;
determining the conformity of proposed coastal de-
velopment with standards of the California Coastal
Act of 1976; assisting local governments in the
preparation of local coastal programs required by
the Coastal Act; reviewing local coastal programs
to determine whether they have been developed and
are being carried out in accordance with State and
Federal laws; and preparing recommendations to the
State Coastal Commission and six regional Coastal
Commissions to guide the Commissions to their for-
mal actions on development permits and appeals,
coastal planning, and local coastal programs.

     This list is not necessarily exhaustive of all the per-
mit history activities in which a permit analyst may persona-
lly and substantially engage that would limit his future
private employment opportunities. Moreover, there may be
exceptions where participation in one of the above work
assignments for a particular project would not prevent future
private employment on the same project. See Section 87403.

Peter Douglas
February 26, 1982
Page 4


Thus since the prohibitions of Sections 87401 and 87402
require a subjective test to be applied in determining possi-
ble violations, each situation must be reviewed on an indi-
vidual basis. Former California Coastal Commission employees
therefore should contact the California Coastal Commission
legal staff for guidance as to what specific permit analyst
activities constitute personal and substantial participation
for purposes of Sections 87401 and 87402. The
Coastal Commission then may look to the FPPC's Legal Division
for assistance in interpreting the intent and definitions of
the terms of these sections. Weighing the facts of each case
in light of the broad policy considerations set forth above
should lead to a correct and fair determination.

     If I can be of any further help in this particular
matter, please do not hesitate to contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Steven R. Crooks
                                        Legal Counsel
                                        Enforcement Division


SRC:sf
cc:Mr. Lynn Heacox
   7071 Betty Drive
   Huntington Beach, CA 92647

631 Howard Street, 4th floor
San Francisco, California 94105
(415) 543-8555

December 15, 1981

Mr. Lynn Heacox
7071 Betty Drive
Huntington Beach, 29647

Dear Lynn:

SUBJECT: Possible Civil and Criminal Liability Resulting from
Involvement in Coastal Permit (nos. P-81-7701,
Ben Johnson Estates; and A-31-7613, MacLeod)

This letter is to notify you that serious questions have been raised
in connection with your representation of permit applicants in matters
pending before the Commission in which you were involved while an
employee with the Coastal Commission. While you have verbally assured
Tim Eichenberg of our legal staff that you will refrain from further
participation in the two permits listed above, it is nevertheless my
obligation under the law to advise you that actions you have already
taken in connection with these permits raised questions of possible
violations of the law. In addition, I want to put in writing your
verbal commitment that you will no longer be involved in the listed
projects. As Tim Eichenberg informed you last week, we are sending a
copy of this letter to the applicants in the above listed permit matters
so that they are on notice that concerns have been raised about your
participation in these items. In doing so, we do not reach any judge-
ment about the legality of the actions in question.

Before I explain the basis for our positions, I want to make absolutely
clear that the Commission has never concerned itself with who an appli-
cant chooses as a representative in permit matters. That is totally
within the discretion of the applicant. Our concern is focused on the
merits of every matter that comes before us and with the integrity of
the Commission's process. In addition, we have an obligation to ensure
that permit actions by the Commission are legally sound. Were we to do
otherwise, we would be doing a disservice to the Commission, the appli-
cant and the public. Having said that, let me explain the basis for our
position.

As you know, AB1049 became effective January 1, 1981. It requires,
in relevant part, that:

No former state administrative official, after the termination
of his or her employment . . ., shall for compensation act as
agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person
. . . before any court or state administrative agency or any
officer or employee there of by making any oral or written
communication with the intent to influence, in connection with

Mr. Lynn Heacox
December 15, 1981
Page Two

any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding . . . if the
proceeding is one in which the former state administrative
official participated.

Gov't Code Section 87401
(emphasis added)

Participated means to have taken part personally and sub-
stantially through decisions, approval, disapproval, formal
written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis,
investigation or use of confidential information as an officer
or employee . . . .

Gov't Code Section 87400(d)
(emphasis added)

The Fair Political Practices Commission, by letter of December 4, 1980,
explained how the provisions of this new law apply to Commission staff.
Copies of this letter were made available to you and all Commission
staff late last year. As you will recall, it was primarily because of
concerns raised by South Coast office staff that we pressed for this
letter from the FPPC, which expressly provides that:

The permit analyst may not accept employment with a permit
applicant which would require the analyst to represent or
advise the applicant in further proceedings before the Com-
mission on that permit. (page one)

An employee may not work on the project if the permit holder
has to appear before the Commission on its staff on that pro-
ject, and the former employee will either represent or advise
him on the appearance. . . . On the conditions which are still
pending before the Commission, the former employee is prohibited
from appearance before the Commission as well as assisting the
permit applicant as to those pending conditions. (page two)

In view of these facts and based on the advice of our legal counsel, it
is my opinion that your activities relative to these permits fall within
the letter and spirit of the prohibitions set forth in Section 97401 of
the Political Reform Act and the FPPC's interpretation of that law.
Because of the serious nature of this matter and the potential penalties
which may result if a violation of law has occurred, I am forwarding a
copy of this letter to the Attorney General's Office for a review of the
facts and a determination whether there have been any violations of law.
Obviously, it will then be up to the appropriate enforcement agencies to
take whatever action they deem appropriate.

On the advice of legal counsel, I have instructed our staff to refrain
from any further communications with you which involve these pending

Mr. Lynn Heacox
December 15, 1981
Page Three

permits or any others with which your were involved during your tenure
with the Commission. You are, of course, free to represent any persons
on any other permit matter in which you were not substantially involved
while employed by the Coastal Commission. Our staff will be glad to
work with you on any such other matters. Finally, I want to add my
support of the advice you received from several members of our legal staff
to check prior to representing anyone or whose permits you worked on while
employed with the Commission. This advise, the letter from the Attorney
General's office citing the appropriate sections of the statute, and the
FPPC letter should have made clear the necessity for careful scrutiny
prior to representing the applicants listed above. A letter from the
FPPC or our legal department prior to such activity would have given
your client and yourself some measure of security and avoided a situation such as this one. I hope that in the future, you will make these
early inquiries.

As I said before Lynn, I regret that this matter has come up and gone
so far. Both as a Commission employee and as a former employee, we have
a duty of care which changes that our actions involving Coastal Act requirements are above reproach and reflect a rigorous adherence to the
highest standards of conduct. The integrity of the Coastal Act and the
public's confidence in our proceedings are at stake.

Please call if you have questions, or wish to discuss this matter further

Sincerely,

PETER DOUGLAS
Chief Deputy Director

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a former California Coastal Commission permit analyst violated Sections 87401 and/or 87402 of the Political Reform Act by representing private parties in permit matters before the Commission, and when such representation would be prohibited if compensation were involved.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The former permit analyst's uncompensated representation activities did not violate the Act because compensation is required for a violation. However, the FPPC provides guidance that a former permit analyst generally cannot accept compensated employment with a private party regarding a development site permit application pending before the Commission when the analyst personally and substantially participated in the prior permit history for that site.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a cleanly structured FPPC advice letter with two pages. No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers present, but content clearly follows advice letter structure with factual background, legal analysis, and advisory guidance. Minor OCR degradation in some sections but core content legible. Document references Government Code Sections 87401-87405 and Political Reform Act provisions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a former California Coastal Commission permit analyst violated Sections 87401 and/or 87402 of the Political Reform Act by representing private parties in permit matters before the Commission, and when such representation would be prohibited if compensation were involved.

CONCLUSION: The former permit analyst's uncompensated representation activities did not violate the Act because compensation is required for a violation. However, the FPPC provides guidance that a former permit analyst generally cannot accept compensated employment with a private party regarding a development site permit application pending before the Commission when the analyst personally and substantially participated in the prior permit history for that site.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responded to an inquiry about whether former California Coastal Commission permit analyst Lynn Heacox violated the Political Reform Act by representing private parties in permit matters. The FPPC determined no violation occurred for uncompensated representation but provided guidance that compensated representation would be prohibited when the former analyst previously participated substantially in matters concerning that same project.
